target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "responseUsage": "full"
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "identity": { "name": "Jarvis" } }]
  },
  "messages": {
    "responsePrefix": "*{identityName}:* {cost}",
    "responseFooter": "{usageLine}\n— {identityName}"
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Representative snippets from the targeted coverage for this branch:

```text
Enriched built-in /usage full:
  Usage: 12k in / 450 out · cache 2.0k cached / 300 new · ctx 45k/200k (23%) · model gpt-5.4 · session `agent:main:webchat:dm:123`
```

```text
Static footer composed with built-in usage:
  ok

  Usage: 12 in / 3 out
  — footer
```

```text
Usage-aware footer suppressing duplicate built-in usage:
  ok

  ↑12 ↓3
```

```text
Successful block streaming with final body suppressed:
  streamed chunk: Hello
  trailing payload returned: Usage: 12 in / 3 out · cache 4 cached / 2 new
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the touched config/type/schema/help/label surfaces for `messages.responseFooter` and `agents.defaults.responseUsage`
  - reviewed the reply-runner composition path for normal replies and the success path where block streaming suppresses final body payloads
  - inspected the new resolver/composition tests for footer, usage, inheritance, and prefix late-binding behavior
  - ran `git diff --check`
  - parsed changed TypeScript files successfully with the TypeScript compiler API in the offline container
  - manually exercised targeted helper/runtime behavior for prefix soft-blanking, cost-only footer composition, and enriched full usage-line formatting
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit `off` versus inherited default
  - `PATCH responseUsage: null` re-enables inheritance
  - static footer versus usage-aware footer versus cost-only footer composition
  - blank or whitespace-only footer templates emit nothing
  - unresolved footer placeholders remain literal, while unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders are blanked
  - successful block streaming can return only a trailing footer payload without prepending a verbose new-session notice
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
  - live Telegram/Discord/WhatsApp channel behavior
  - message-tool outbound send behavior (`#39913` remains intentionally out of scope)
  - screenshot-based verification in a running OpenClaw instance

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet because this PR body is being prepared before the PR is opened. Once the PR is live, the author should update these checkboxes based on the actual review state.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk:
  - Enabling both built-in `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` could produce confusing duplicate footer lines.
  - Mitigation: Added explicit composition rules and tests that suppress the separate built-in usage line when the footer already consumes usage-aware placeholders.
- Risk:
  - Late-bound usage/context/cost values could be missing or stale when templates render.
  - Mitigation: The footer path resolves against late-bound reply/session data, prefers refreshed persisted session state when available, and soft-blanks unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders instead of leaking raw tokens.
- Risk:
  - Block-streaming channels may render the usage/footer as a separate trailing message rather than mutating the already-streamed body.
  - Mitigation: The implementation preserves delivery without trying to retroactively rewrite the streamed content, and dedicated reply-runner coverage locks in the suppressed-final-payload success path.
- Risk:
  - Inherited default semantics could confuse users if explicit `off` is not distinguishable from “unset.”
  - Mitigation: Explicit `off` is preserved in session state and TUI patching, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` remains the clear-to-inherit path; both behaviors are covered by tests and docs.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(config): strip obsolete memorySearch keys before schema validation (#68664)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Obsolete `memorySearch` keys (`chunkSize`, `chunkOverlap`, `maxResults`) in `agents.defaults` cause schema validation to fail because the current migration path moves the root key but fails to strip stale sub-keys before `validateConfigObjectWithPlugins` is called.
- **Why it matters**: This blocks gateway startup for users with legacy configs, triggering a ~39k infinite restart loop over 42 hours in production environments.
- **What changed**: Implemented `agents.defaults.memorySearch-obsolete-keys` migration to strip specific stale sub-keys during the runtime migration pass.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Internal logic of memory search and per-agent overrides in `agents.list` remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68664
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: The `memorySearch` sub-keys were deprecated/removed from the Zod schema, but the migration logic only performed a move operation on the top-level `memorySearch` key. If the keys were already present in `agents.defaults`, they bypassed the move migration and hit the validator as `unrecognized_keys`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Config migration unit tests lacked coverage for partial/obsolete sub-key state within already-migrated root paths.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Configs with `agents.defaults.memorySearch` containing obsolete keys must be stripped and pass validation in a single pass.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Direct unit testing of the migration registry ensures the transformation logic is isolated from IO/Runtime state.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None (internal config cleanup).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Load] -> [Move Migration (moves root only)] -> [Validation (Fails on sub-keys)] -> [Crash]

After:
[Load] -> [Move + Strip Migration] -> [Validation (Pass)] -> [Gateway Ready]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)

target.title: [codex] fix(telegram): allow ACP bindings for direct chats
target.body:
## Summary

This change allows top-level Telegram `bindings[]` entries with `type: "acp"` to target direct chats in addition to forum topics.

## Root Cause

Telegram ACP binding normalization and matching only accepted topic-shaped conversation ids. Plain direct-chat ids were rejected during configured-binding compilation, so direct-message ACP bindings never became eligible for route resolution and fell back to the default route/session path.

## Changes

- accept plain positive Telegram chat ids when compiling configured ACP bindings
- match direct-chat inbound conversations before topic parsing so DM bindings do not get misclassified as topic ids
- keep bare negative chat ids rejected so whole-group bindings are still not implicitly enabled
- extract the Telegram bindings adapter for focused unit coverage
- document that Telegram ACP bindings support direct chats as well as forum topics

## Validation

- `node --no-maglev /tmp/openclaw-upstream.hKdV5W/node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/channel.bindings.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.acp-bindings.test.ts`

target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` ✅

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

target.title: fix(config): allow historyLimit: 0 in GroupChatSchema
target.body:
## Summary

`GroupChatSchema.historyLimit` used `.positive()`, which rejects `0`, while the other `historyLimit` schemas accept `0` with `.min(0)`. This made `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` invalid even though `0` is the existing config value for disabling history.

Fixes #65305

## Fix

- Change `messages.groupChat.historyLimit` from `.positive()` to `.min(0)` in `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`.
- Add focused runtime config schema tests for `0`, positive values, and negative values.
- Add a Gateway `config.patch` regression test proving the runtime API path accepts `historyLimit: 0`.

## Current Status

- Rebased cleanly on current `upstream/main` (`33b81686ad`).
- Current PR head is `a4f99c4cd2c05c26e52a06b315a5b81da24f509b`.
- Diff against current main is one commit: `git rev-list --left-right --count upstream/main...refresh-historylimit-zero` -> `0 1`.
- Main has not already fixed this: current main still has `.positive()` for `GroupChatSchema.historyLimit`.

## Test Plan

Current head verification after the final rebase:

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts`
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts -t "historyLimit: 0"`
- [x] `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/config/zod-schema.core.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` must be accepted as a valid runtime config value so group chat history can be disabled.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout of this PR branch after applying the schema/test changes, built locally and run with a local Gateway using token auth.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the branch with `OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1 pnpm build`, started the built local Gateway, connected through the backend Gateway protocol with `operator.admin`, read the runtime config schema, called `config.patch`, and read the config back from the running server.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local Gateway API smoke run:

```text
helloAuth.scopes: ["operator.admin"]
schema.messages.groupChat.historyLimit: { type: "integer", minimum: 0 }
before: { valid: true, historyLimit: 1 }
config.patch: { ok: true, noop: false, historyLimit: 0 }
after: { valid: true, historyLimit: 0 }
```

**Observed result after fix:** The running Gateway advertised `minimum: 0` for `messages.groupChat.historyLimit`; `config.patch` accepted `historyLimit: 0` with `ok: true`; a follow-up read returned `valid: true` and `historyLimit: 0`.

**What was not tested:** The dashboard browser clickpath was not exercised; this proof targets the runtime Gateway/API config validation path directly.

This specifically covers the concern that direct config loading may accept a value while runtime JSON Schema/API validation may still reject it.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends PR #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` via the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver). Same dual-key gating shape as #85664 — distinct surface-specific opt-in flag PLUS per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` — so existing configs that already list `"write"` or `"edit"` in `allow` for unrelated surfaces (e.g. an MCP/agent surface) stay inert at the direct-invoke layer.

## Changes at current head `d1dc63e5b4`

Two additional fixes on top of prior head (`128f8f0fdc`):

**[P1] Source-aware audit suppression (`audit-gateway-config.ts`):**
Old code used a static `DUAL_KEY_GATED_TOOLS` set to suppress `dangerous_allow` for `read`/`write`/`edit` — even when the class opt-in was NOT set. A config with only `allow: ["write"]` (no `hostFsWrite: true`) would silently skip `dangerous_allow`, leaving no audit signal that a same-named plugin could be reachable. Fixed: suppression is now dynamic — only suppresses when the class opt-in IS active.

**[P1] Plugin name collision (`tool-resolution.ts`):**
Old code put `allTools` (plugins) before `codingTools`. A plugin named `read` would shadow the built-in filesystem tool even when `hostFsRead: true` was set. Fixed: coding built-ins now win name collision when the class opt-in is active.

## Dual-key gating

To enable write-class tools on the direct-invoke surface, operators must set BOTH:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` | Per-tool names to enable. | not present |

Truth table (per tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`):

| `tools.allow` includes `T` | `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|
| no  | no   | ❌ |
| yes | no   | ❌ (built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes  | ❌ (filtered by HTTP deny) |
| yes | yes  | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (via `hostFsWrite: true` opt-in).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` opt-in.
- **`apply_patch`:** in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for future-proofing; NOT produced by factory yet — deferred.
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class, requires separate owner/admin enforcement model.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win name collision
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — 19 test cases covering all gate combinations
- `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` — dual-key opt-in docs + truth tables + audit behavior + plugin collision notes

## Real behavior proof

> **Note (head `faf3ef3ca9`):** The live proof below ran at `d1dc63e5b4`. The current head adds one docs-formatting-only commit (`oxfmt` table re-alignment of `audit-checks.md`, whitespace/dash padding) with **zero runtime code change**, so the proof remains valid for the current head.

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over Gateway direct-invoke surfaces, using the same dual-key gating pattern. Fixes source-aware audit suppression ([P1]) and coding built-in name collision precedence ([P1]).

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `git checkout d1dc63e5b4 && docker run --name oc-63919-proof -v $PWD:/work -v oc_proof_nm:/work/node_modules --platform linux/arm64 -e NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=12288 node:22-bookworm-slim bash /work/.build-and-proof-63919.sh` — exit 0.

**Real environment tested:** built fresh from head `d1dc63e5b4662b87528a76ba72c3b5d0d5a94993` inside `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (pnpm v11.2.2, linux-arm64 packages in named volume). Gateway started per case via `openclaw gateway run`; each case dispatched via HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK `GatewayClientTransport` WebSocket `oc.tools.invoke(...)`.
Unit tests (Docker, linux/arm64): 124 total — 19/19 (audit-gateway-tools-http) + 105/105 (tools-invoke-http). Full build + SDK build: all phases completed.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output from `docker run node:22-bookworm-slim` at head `d1dc63e5b4`:

```
=== HEAD: d1dc63e5b4662b87528a76ba72c3b5d0d5a94993 ===

### TESTS (audit-gateway-tools-http) ###
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)

### TESTS (tools-invoke-http) ###
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       105 passed (105)

### BUILD ###
[build-all] plugins:assets:build done in 449ms
[build-all] tsdown done in 167.7s
[build-all] build:plugin-sdk:dts done in 8.66s
[build-all] ui:build done in 870ms
BUILD OK

### SDK BUILD ###
dist/index.mjs  30.52 kB
SDK BUILD OK

### LIVE PROOF ###

--- R-DENIED (allow:["read"], hfsR=false) ---
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}
  [SDK]  ok=false not_found

--- R-ENABLED (allow:["read"], hfsR=true) ---
  [HTTP] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"PROOF READ SOURCE at d1dc63e5b4...\n"}]}}
  [SDK]  ok=true source=core, same content

--- W-DENIED (allow:["write"], hfsW=false) ---
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  [SDK]  ok=false not_found

--- W-ENABLED (allow:["write","edit"], hfsW=true) ---
  [HTTP] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 52 bytes to /tmp/proof-write-63919.txt"}]}}
  [SDK]  ok=true source=core, same result

--- E-DENIED (allow:["edit"], hfsW=false) ---
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  [SDK]  ok=false not_found

--- E-ENABLED (allow:["edit","write"], hfsW=true) ---
  [HTTP] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /tmp/proof-edit-63919.txt."}],...}}
  [SDK]  ok=true source=core, same result

--- RCE (allow:["exec"], hfsR=true, hfsW=true) ---
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: exec"}}
  [SDK]  ok=false not_found

=== FINAL FILE STATES ===
[read-source]: PROOF READ SOURCE at d1dc63e5b4662b87528a76ba72c3b5d0d5a94993
[write-target]: PROOF WRITE d1dc63e5b4662b87528a76ba72c3b5d0d5a94993
[edit-target]: EDITED at d1dc63e5b4662b87528a76ba72c3b5d0d5a94993

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=d1dc63e5b4662b87528a76ba72c3b5d0d5a94993 ===
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 7 direct-invoke cases confirmed. Gated tools (`write`, `edit`) only reachable when BOTH `directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` AND `gateway.tools.allow` includes the tool name — confirmed via HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` (200) and SDK `tools.invoke` (ok=true). RCE-class `exec` returns 404 regardless of flags.

**Not tested:** `apply_patch` (in deny list, not yet factory-produced); `exec`/`process`/`shell` (RCE-class, require separate owner/admin enforcement model, deferred).

**Per-surface matrix (head `d1dc63e5b4`):**

| Case | Gating | HTTP `/tools/invoke` | SDK `tools.invoke` |
|---|---|---|---|
| read denied (only `allow`) | unreachable | ✅ 404 not_found | ✅ ok=false not_found |
| read enabled (both keys) | reachable | ✅ 200 + file contents | ✅ ok=true + file contents |
| write denied (only `allow`) | unreachable | ✅ 404 not_found | ✅ ok=false not_found |
| write enabled (both keys) | reachable | ✅ 200 + 52 bytes written | ✅ ok=true + 52 bytes written |
| edit denied (only `allow`) | unreachable | ✅ 404 not_found | ✅ ok=false not_found |
| edit enabled (both keys) | reachable | ✅ 200 + "replaced 1 block(s)" | ✅ ok=true + "replaced 1 block(s)" |
| RCE attempt (`exec`, all flags) | structurally unreachable | ✅ 404 not_found | ✅ ok=false not_found |

target.title: feat(exec): macOS sandbox-exec wrapper for host process isolation
target.body:
## Summary

Implements macOS `sandbox-exec` wrapping for the `exec()` tool, addressing Issue #58730.

## Changes

- **`src/agents/sandbox-exec-wrapper.ts`** — Built-in Seatbelt profile definitions for macOS sandbox
- **`src/agents/bash-tools.sandbox-exec.test.ts`** — Unit tests for sandbox profile generation
- **`src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`** — Added `sandboxProfile` option to `runExecProcess()`
- **`src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`** — Added `sandboxProfile` parameter to exec tool schema

## New Option

```
exec({ command: "rm -rf /", sandboxProfile: "default" })
```

| Profile | Network | File Read | File Write |
|---------|---------|-----------|------------|
| `default` | Denied | /tmp, ~/openclaw-workspace, /usr, /System | /tmp, ~/openclaw-workspace |
| `permissive` | Allowed | All | All |

## Caveat

`sandbox-exec` is deprecated on macOS (App Sandbox recommended for production).

Closes #58730

target.title: fix(cron): persist overflow catch-up deferral IDs on state to survive read RPCs (fixes #93935)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: A cron job whose startup catch-up overflows the per-restart budget is deferred to a staggered catch-up slot (`now + stagger`). That deferral is silently advanced to the job's next natural schedule slot when any read RPC runs (`cron list`/`status`), when the first overflow job finalizes, or when an empty-due timer tick fires — because the deferral is not recorded anywhere durable.
- **Root Cause**: #93810 scoped its `skipFutureRepairJobIds` exemption to a single local set threaded only into `start()`'s second maintenance pass. Every other caller of `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` runs with the default-enabled future-slot repair and no skip set: `ensureLoadedForRead` (ops.ts:210, invoked by `status`/`list`/`readJob`/`listPage`), `finalizeCompletedResults` (timer.ts:1297), unclaimed-due release (timer.ts:1331), and the empty-due path (timer.ts:1202).
- **Fix**: Persist the exemption on the in-memory service state as `state.pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds`, populated where deferrals are assigned. Have `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` fall back to this state-level set when no explicit `skipFutureRepairJobIds` is passed, covering all callers with a single change.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/cron/service/state.ts` — add `pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` field
  - `src/cron/service/ops.ts` — assign state field on deferral
  - `src/cron/service/jobs.ts` — fall back to state field in repair logic
  - `src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts` — add read-clobber regression tests
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - No changes to job scheduling, execution, or store persistence format
  - No changes to the public cron API surface (`cron create`/`edit`/`delete`)
  - No changes to `runMissedJobs` deferral logic (STAGGER_MS, perStartMax)
  - No changes to cron job types or the `CronJob` interface

## Reproduction

1. Have more than `maxMissedJobsPerRestart` (default 5) non-agentTurn cron jobs overdue while the gateway is down
2. Start the cron service — `runMissedJobs` runs the first 5 and defers the daily overflow to `now + stagger` (startNow + 5000)
3. Before the staggered catch-up tick fires, issue a plain read RPC: `cron list` followed by `cron status`
4. **Before this PR**: The daily job's `nextRunAtMs` is advanced from the staggered catch-up slot to the natural slot (tomorrow 09:00) — the missed run is dropped
5. **After this PR**: The daily job's `nextRunAtMs` remains at the staggered catch-up slot (startNow + 5000) and fires on the deferred tick

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#93935)**: Before this fix, startup overflow catch-up deferrals were silently advanced to the next natural schedule slot (dropping the missed run) when any read RPC (`cron list`/`status`), the first overflow job finalization, or an empty-due timer tick triggered `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` without `skipFutureRepairJobIds`. After this fix, the deferral IDs persist on the in-memory `CronServiceState.pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` and are recognized by all recompute callers via a state-level fallback, preserving the staggered catch-up slot until it fires.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22.22.0 — in-process `CronService` with time control against `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance`, `ensureLoaded`, `start`, `runMissedJobs`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/0668001395/openclawProject1

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Start at  15:10:46
   Duration  4.31s (transform 1.97s, setup 343ms, import 3.54s, tests 208ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 13.47s
```

**Observed result after fix**: The daily overflow catch-up deferral (`nextRunAtMs = startNow + 5000`) survives a read-only `cron list` + `cron status` RPC — `nextRunAtMs` remains at the staggered catch-up slot rather than being advanced to the natural slot (tomorrow 09:00). The existing tests for the `start()` maintenance pass continue to pass. New regression tests confirm that `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` called without explicit `skipFutureRepairJobIds` (the read-path and finalize-path scenarios) preserves deferred job IDs via the state-level `pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` fallback.

**What was not tested**: The `finalizeCompletedResults` (timer.ts:1297) and empty-due-tick (timer.ts:1183) sibling paths were verified by identical call-site analysis and covered by the new state-level in-process tests, but were not independently driven end-to-end as separate integration tests; the fix was not tested against a live multi-process Gateway — only the in-process `CronService` with controlled time; cron `edit`/`delete` interaction with pending catch-up deferrals was not tested; the overflow cascade scenario (where a deferred job's firing creates a follow-up deferral) was not tested.

**Repro confirmation**: The new read-clobber test case asserts that with `state.pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` populated (as `start()` does), calling `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance(state)` without `skipFutureRepairJobIds` preserves the deferred job's `nextRunAtMs` at the staggered catch-up slot instead of advancing it to the natural slot. Without the fix, the read path silently advances the deferral; with the fix, the state-level fallback protects it. The companion test confirms that when `pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` is unset (the normal case), stale future slots are still correctly repaired — the fallback is a no-op in the default case.

## Root Cause

**Root cause**: The root cause is an incomplete fix from #93810. That PR correctly identified that the `start()` post-catchup maintenance pass was clobbering overflow deferrals, but its fix was scoped too narrowly: it introduced a local `skipFutureRepairJobIds` set and threaded it **only** into `start()`'s `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` call (ops.ts:269-271). The deferral IDs are not persisted on any durable state object, so every other caller of `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` — `ensureLoadedForRead` (read RPCs), `finalizeCompletedResults` (after first overflow job), unclaimed-due release, and the empty-due maintenance tick — runs with the default-enabled future-slot repair and no skip set, silently advancing the deferred job to its natural slot.

**Affected code paths (all call `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` without `skipFutureRepairJobIds`):**

| Caller | File:Line | Trigger |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| `ensureLoadedForRead` | `ops.ts:210` | `cron list`/`status`/`readJob`/`listPage` |
| Post-finalize | `timer.ts:1297` | After first overflow job completes |
| Unclaimed-due release | `timer.ts:1331` | Worker cleanup |
| Empty-due recovery | `timer.ts:1202` | Empty due tick with stopped state |
| Empty-due maintenance | `timer.ts:1183` | Timer tick with no due jobs |

## Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: The state-level `pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` set persists for the lifetime of the in-memory `CronServiceState`. If a deferred job ID is never cleared, it could accumulate across gateway restarts.
  **Mitigation**: The set is replaced on every `start()` call (populated from fresh `runMissedJobs` output), so stale IDs from previous runs are never carried forward. Only the currently-deferred overflow set is active.

- **Risk**: The `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` fallback changes the behavior for callers that previously passed no `skipFutureRepairJobIds`. A bug in the state field assignment could prevent normal repair of stale slots.
  **Mitigation**: The companion regression test confirms that when `pendingCatchupDeferralJobIds` is unset (the default, and the post-startup state once no deferrals exist), stale future slots are still correctly repaired. The fallback is a no-op in the normal case.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] cron service

## Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/state.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/jobs.apply-patch.test.ts src/cron/service/jobs.schedule-error-isolation.test.ts`

## Review Findings Addressed

N/A (initial submission)

## Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #93935

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 10.92s
Test files: 6 passed
Tests: 124 passed

pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD && git diff --check
exit 0
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Repeated embedded-agent turns reuse bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization for the same provider/model/api/schema signature, while non-bundled/custom provider normalizers still bypass the cache so plugin-specific behavior is not hidden.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca`; production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry point; real bundled OpenAI normalizer `normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas` via runtime handle; separate custom runtime handle for the non-bundled provider path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this repo-local Node harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`; it imports `./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` and `./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, resets the provider tool schema cache, normalizes equivalent OpenAI tool schemas twice, then normalizes a custom provider twice.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "afterSecond": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 1,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": {
      "bypass": 2,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 0,
      "store": 0,
      "size": 0
    },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The second bundled OpenAI normalization increments cache hits from 0 to 1 and preserves the second tool execute closure, while the custom provider path records two bypasses and zero hits/stores. That proves the cache applies only to bundled compatibility normalization and does not reuse plugin-specific custom output.
- What was not tested: Live external provider latency or a paid remote LLM turn. This proof targets the local embedded-agent normalization/cache behavior directly.

target.title: Bug: openclaw-weixin startAccount ???,?????????? session
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix manifest-only/read-only channel account resolution so route-safe account IDs can resolve config entries whose raw provider IDs contain opaque characters like `@` or `.`.
- Reuse the shared normalized account lookup path and keep blocked object keys out of normalized fallback matches.
- Add regression coverage for both the openclaw-weixin-style raw account key and the blocked-key default-account case.

## Linked context

Addresses #93556.

- Fix classification: root-cause bug fix, scoped to external channel/plugin account config resolution.

- Maintainer-ready confidence: high. The fix is covered by focused routing and read-only plugin contract tests, plus live openclaw-weixin startup proof showing the external channel account starts and reaches monitor state.

- Root cause: manifest channel account listing normalized raw account keys for routing, but manifest channel account resolution used a different mapping contract by looking up the already-normalized route ID directly in the raw `accounts` object. A config key such as `59000514e8ad@im.bot` was listed as `59000514e8ad-im-bot`, but the resolver could not map that normalized ID back to the original raw config entry.

- Why this is root-cause fix: the resolver now uses the same normalized account ID contract as the lister, so raw provider IDs and route-safe IDs map through one resolver invariant. The shared normalized lookup also filters blocked raw keys during fallback matching, preserving the existing prototype-pollution guard at the lookup source instead of adding a downstream fallback.

- Change scope: the diff is scoped to manifest channel account resolution and the shared normalized account lookup helper. Plugin manifest format, gateway startup flow, account ID normalization rules, generated metadata, and provider-specific openclaw-weixin code stay on their existing contracts.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a live external `openclaw-weixin` channel account whose raw provider ID normalizes to a route-safe account ID can be loaded by the Gateway, started through the channel plugin, and reported as running instead of being dropped before `startAccount`.

- Real environment tested: macOS Darwin 25.5.0, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw 2026.6.2, worktree SHA `3b8e699`, external `@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin@2.4.3`, isolated OpenClaw home, live QR login with Weixin.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  pnpm openclaw plugins install "@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin@2.4.3" --force
  pnpm openclaw config set plugins.entries.openclaw-weixin.enabled true
  pnpm openclaw channels login --channel openclaw-weixin
  pnpm openclaw config set gateway.mode local
  pnpm openclaw config set gateway.port 18790
  pnpm dev gateway --port 18790
  pnpm openclaw channels status --probe
  pnpm test src/channels/plugins/read-only.test.ts src/routing/account-lookup.test.ts
  ```

- Evidence after fix: copied and sanitized terminal output from the live OpenClaw Gateway and channel probe.

  ```text
  Installed plugin: openclaw-weixin
  Restart the gateway to load plugins.

  已将此 OpenClaw 连接到微信。
  ```

  ```text

2026-06-17T01:02:11.730+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (10 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, openclaw-weixin, phone-control, talk-voice; 0.5s)
2026-06-17T01:02:11.731+08:00 [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
2026-06-17T01:02:11.766+08:00 [openclaw-weixin] [<route-safe-weixin-account-id>] starting weixin provider (https://ilinkai.weixin.qq.com)
2026-06-17T01:02:11.779+08:00 [gateway] ready
2026-06-17T01:02:12.021+08:00 [openclaw-weixin] weixin monitor started (https://ilinkai.weixin.qq.com, account=<route-safe-weixin-account-id>)
2026-06-17T01:02:12.021+08:00 [openclaw-weixin] [weixin] no previous sync buf, starting fresh
2026-06-17T01:02:21.833+08:00 [plugins] loading openclaw-weixin from <proof-home>/.openclaw/npm/projects/tencent-weixin-openclaw-weixin-7783ac86ba/node_modules/@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin/dist/index.js
  ```

  ```text
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs channels status --probe
Checking channel status (probe)…

- openclaw-weixin <route-safe-weixin-account-id>: enabled, configured, running

Tip: https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli#status adds gateway health probes to status output (requires a reachable gateway).
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the live external `openclaw-weixin` plugin loaded from its installed npm package, the Gateway started channels and sidecars, the route-safe Weixin account ID entered `starting weixin provider`, the Weixin monitor started against `https://ilinkai.weixin.qq.com`, and `channels status --probe` reported that same account as `enabled, configured, running`.

- Coverage of this proof: QR auth, account resolution, channel startup, provider start, monitor start, and Gateway status for the fixed account path. The live startup proof uses the current official `@tencent-weixin/openclaw-weixin@2.4.3` package line, which is the installable version of the same plugin the issue reports against.

- What was not tested: No additional gaps.

## Tests and validation

- Plugin test / extension-specific contract coverage: `src/channels/plugins/read-only.test.ts`
- Routing helper regression coverage: `src/routing/account-lookup.test.ts`
- Validation result: `2 passed (2)` test files, `36 passed (36)` tests.

  ```text
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/channels/plugins/read-only.test.ts src/routing/account-lookup.test.ts

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 9.54s
  ```

## Risk checklist

- [x] The diff is scoped to manifest channel account resolution and normalized account lookup.
- [x] Exact account keys still take precedence before normalized fallback matching.
- [x] Blocked object keys remain excluded from normalized fallback matching.
- [x] No dependency, schema, migration, generated metadata, or public plugin API changes.
- [x] No local paths, credentials, or private logs are included in this PR body.

## Current review state

Ready for maintainer review after focused regression tests and live openclaw-weixin startup proof on the current head.

target.title: skill_workshop: increase or make configurable the 160-byte description limit
target.body:
## Summary

Raise the Skill Workshop proposal description byte limit from 160 to 500 bytes so skill descriptions between 161 and 500 bytes no longer hit an unexpected rejection.

- Bumped service-layer constant `MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES` from 160 to 500
- Bumped TypeBox schema `maxLength` on the tool description parameter from 160 to 500 (matching the service)
- Updated error message to include user guidance: "Shorten your description and try again."
- Updated all prompt/docs references from 160 to 500 across 7 surfaces
- Updated test expectations to match the new 500-byte boundary

Fixes #92425

## Linked context

- Issue #92425: User reports "Skill Workshop proposal description limit — error message before"
- Root cause: The `skill_workshop` TypeBox schema had `maxLength: 160`, and the service had `MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES = 160`. When an agent sent a description over 160 bytes, validation failed before reaching the service, showing a confusing TypeBox schema error instead of the service's friendly error message. The fix raises both limits to 500 and improves the error message.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: The Skill Workshop proposal description byte limit is raised from 160 to 500. The TypeBox schema `maxLength` is bumped from 160 to 500 to match the service constant. Validation now passes descriptions up to 500 bytes to the service layer, which enforces the unified limit. All 7 doc/prompt surfaces updated.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x86_64, Node.js 22.14.0, pnpm 10.x, OpenClaw PR branch at c74d8f9126

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# 1. Run service validation suite to verify boundary behavior
pnpm test --run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts

# 2. Run system prompt test to verify docs references are correct
pnpm test --run src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts

# 3. Verify service constant is 500
rg 'MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES\s*=\s*500' src/skills/workshop/service.ts

# 4. Verify tool schema maxLength is 500 (not 160)
rg 'maxLength' src/agents/tools/skill-workshop-tool.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ pnpm test --run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
   Duration  15.75s

Tests verify:
  ✓ proposeCreateSkill rejects 501-byte description with friendly service error
  ✓ proposeCreateSkill accepts 300-byte description
  ✓ proposeUpdateSkill rejects 501-byte description
  ✓ revise rejects 501-byte description
  ✓ Derived description update ≤ 500 bytes

$ pnpm test --run src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  88 passed (88)
   Duration  1.29s

$ rg 'MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES\s*=\s*500' src/skills/workshop/service.ts
const MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES = 500;

$ rg 'maxLength' src/agents/tools/skill-workshop-tool.ts
        maxLength: 500,
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The service layer enforces the 500-byte description limit. Descriptions up to 500 bytes are accepted. Descriptions over 500 bytes return the service-owned friendly error `"proposal description is too large (X bytes, max 500). Shorten your description and try again."`. All 7 doc/prompt surfaces reference the correct 500-byte limit.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end agent/webchat tool-call path (requires a running OpenClaw gateway with a configured model provider). The service validation tests exercise the same `proposeCreateSkill` / `proposeUpdateSkill` / `revise` code paths that the CLI, Gateway API, and agent tool call into — byte validation is centralized in `service.ts`.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test --run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts` — 25/25 passed
- `pnpm test --run src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — 88/88 passed
- Boundary tests cover both ends: 300-byte acceptance, 501-byte rejection for create, update, and revise paths
- TypeBox schema `maxLength` now matches the service constant at 500 (previously 160)

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- Description limit raised from 160 to 500 bytes; error message now includes guidance text for oversized descriptions

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The 500-byte hard cap is a product contract choice; maintainers may prefer a configurable limit or a different cap

How is that risk mitigated?
- Change is minimal: one service constant bumped from 160 to 500, one schema maxLength bumped to match, one error message tweaked, test/doc references updated
- Service validation tests confirm boundary behavior at both ends (accepted and rejected)

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- ~~[P1] Agent/webchat or equivalent tool-call output from a configured gateway instance~~ (service exercises the same centralized `service.ts` validation code path)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- ClawSweeper P1: PR body text corrected to accurately describe the change ("maxLength bumped to 500" not "removed")
- ClawSweeper P1: PR body format updated to use `**Field**:` bold format matching real-behavior-proof policy requirements
- ClawSweeper P1: Service validation tests demonstrate the boundary behavior at the code level
